subject: Product Management
title: Netflix's Triple Pivot
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Netflix's Triple Pivot.

hook.text: Netflix has found product-market fit not once but three times — DVD rental, DVD subscription, and streaming.

buildup.text: Netflix started as a DVD-by-mail store in 1997. Early growth was slow. Paying per rental felt like Blockbuster online.

discovery.text: The subscription model changed everything: unlimited DVDs for a flat monthly fee. Customers loved the predictability.

twist.text: Then Reed Hastings saw broadband adoption rising. He bet the entire company on streaming — killing the DVD business.

climax.text: Each pivot found new PMF by recognizing market shifts early and adapting the product before competitors could react.

punchline.text: PMF isn't forever. Great companies find it again and again.

quiz.question: What does Netflix's history teach about PMF?
options: PMF must be found multiple times as markets change
Once you find PMF, it lasts forever
Pivoting is always a sign of failure
